agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Order lifecycle management — specifically the cancellation policy governing when and whether a placed order can be reversed before fulfillment.","bounded_context":"Order Management / Order Lifecycle","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Order","definition":"A customer's committed request to purchase goods, tracked through a lifecycle from placement to delivery."},{"term":"Cancellation","definition":"The act of reversing a placed order, returning it to a terminal non-fulfilled state."},{"term":"Invoice","definition":"A financial document generated to lock in pricing and record the obligation to pay. Described by Finance as irreversible once created."},{"term":"Shipment","definition":"The physical dispatch of goods to the customer, representing the fulfillment threshold after which PM agrees cancellation is no longer possible."},{"term":"Order Placement","definition":"The initial act of creating a confirmed order. Operations states invoicing occurs at this moment."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"An order can be cancelled at any time before it ships.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a business policy from the PM defining the cancellation window. A VP could narrow or widen this window.","testable_assertion":"Given an order in any pre-shipment state, when cancellation is requested, then the cancellation succeeds."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Orders that have been invoiced cannot be cancelled; invoicing is irreversible.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Finance treats the invoice as a hard financial constraint — once an invoice exists, the financial record cannot be unwound via simple cancellation. This is a data/financial integrity invariant.","testable_assertion":"Given an order with an associated invoice, when cancellation is requested, then the cancellation is rejected."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"Orders are invoiced at the time of placement to lock in pricing.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is an operational policy decision about when invoicing occurs. It could be changed to invoice at shipment or at a different lifecycle stage.","testable_assertion":"Given a new order is placed, when placement completes, then an invoice record exists for that order."}],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Customer places an order and immediately requests cancellation","expected_behavior":"CONTRADICTED: PM says this should succeed (pre-shipment). Finance says it must fail (already invoiced per Ops policy). Behavior is undefined until contradiction is resolved."},{"scenario":"Order is placed but shipment is delayed for days","expected_behavior":"CONTRADICTED: The entire window between placement and shipment is simultaneously cancellable (PM) and non-cancellable (Finance + Ops)."},{"scenario":"Partial shipment — some items shipped, some not","expected_behavior":"Ambiguous even without the core contradiction. Would cancellation apply to unshipped line items only?"},{"scenario":"Invoice is voided or credit-noted rather than cancelled","expected_behavior":"Potential resolution path: if Finance distinguishes between 'cancelling an invoice' (forbidden) and 'issuing a credit note against an invoice' (allowed), the contradiction may dissolve. But this was not stated."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"If we implement PM's rule and ignore Finance, we risk orphaned invoices in the ledger, revenue recognition errors, and potential audit failures. If we implement Finance's rule, customers effectively cannot cancel any order, leading to refund disputes, chargebacks, and customer churn.","operational":"Operations invoices at placement to lock pricing. Any resolution that delays invoicing disrupts their pricing-lock workflow. Any resolution that allows cancellation of invoiced orders disrupts Finance's ledger integrity.","user":"Customers expect to cancel orders before shipment. If the system silently prevents this due to immediate invoicing, it will generate support tickets, complaints, and reputational damage.","risk":"Shipping either interpretation without resolving the contradiction guarantees one team's invariant is violated. High risk of financial misstatement or customer-facing defect.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers","impact":"Cannot cancel orders if Finance rule wins — leads to frustration, chargebacks, and churn."},{"group":"Finance / Accounting","impact":"Ledger integrity compromised if invoiced orders are cancelled without proper financial reversal mechanism."},{"group":"Operations","impact":"Must change invoicing timing if the resolution delays invoicing to shipment, breaking their pricing-lock strategy."},{"group":"Product Management","impact":"Core feature (pre-shipment cancellation) is blocked until stakeholders align on the business rule."},{"group":"Customer Support","impact":"Will bear the burden of confused customers regardless of which rule is implemented."}]},"confidence":{"level":35,"reasoning":"Three authoritative sources provide requirements that form a logical contradiction: (1) cancel before shipment is allowed, (2) invoiced orders cannot be cancelled, (3) invoicing happens at placement. Combined: no order can ever be cancelled, directly contradicting requirement (1). No acceptance criteria can be defined until the stakeholders resolve this. Confidence is low because the spec is internally inconsistent — any implementation would violate at least one stated constraint.","high_confidence_areas":["The contradiction is real and not a matter of interpretation — it is a logical impossibility to satisfy all three rules simultaneously","The domain terms (order, invoice, shipment, cancellation) are well-understood"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether Finance would accept a credit-note/void mechanism as distinct from 'cancellation'","Whether Operations would accept delaying invoicing to a later lifecycle stage","The intended resolution path — we cannot guess which rule the business would relax"],"assumptions":["Invoice in Finance's context and invoice in Operations' context refer to the same financial artifact","'Cancelled' in PM's language and 'cancelled' in Finance's language mean the same thing","There is no unstated credit-note or reversal mechanism that Finance considers distinct from cancellation"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"The three requirements form a logical impossibility. PM says orders are cancellable before shipment. Finance says invoiced orders are never cancellable. Operations says orders are invoiced at placement. Therefore: every order is invoiced the moment it is placed, making every order non-cancellable from the instant it exists — directly contradicting PM's cancellation policy. These three rules cannot coexist.","affected_stakeholders":["Product Management","Finance","Operations","Customers","Customer Support"],"options":["(A) Delay invoicing until shipment — preserves PM's cancellation window but breaks Operations' pricing-lock strategy. Operations must find another mechanism to lock pricing.","(B) Allow cancellation of invoiced orders via credit note/void — preserves PM's cancellation window and keeps Operations' invoicing timing, but requires Finance to accept a reversal mechanism they currently say is impossible.","(C) Remove pre-shipment cancellation — honors Finance and Operations but eliminates the PM's feature entirely. Customers cannot cancel.","(D) Introduce a 'pro-forma invoice' or 'pricing reservation' at placement that is not a true invoice, deferring the binding invoice to shipment — preserves all three intents by redefining the artifact created at placement."],"recommendation":"Option D is the most likely correct domain resolution. Many logistics and e-commerce businesses distinguish between a pricing reservation or pro-forma invoice (created at order placement to lock pricing) and a binding fiscal invoice (created at or near shipment). If Operations' intent is to lock pricing rather than to create an irreversible financial document, then the artifact at placement is not the same 'invoice' Finance is referring to. However, this MUST be confirmed with all three stakeholders before any implementation begins."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Requirements as stated are internally contradictory and cannot be implemented as a coherent system.","business_rule_broken":"BR-1 is logically negated by the combination of BR-2 and BR-3. Implementing any two rules necessarily violates the third."}]}
